abstract: Since taking office in 2010, the Hungarian government, led by the populist radical right Fidesz party, has gained international notoriety for restructuring domestic institutions to serve an illiberal political agenda. This systematic democratic backsliding has placed Hungary on a collision course with the European Union, culminating in the European Commission triggering its new rule-of-law conditionality mechanism in April 2022. By applying a model of cross-sectoral bargaining, this article argues that the EU’s standoff with Hungary over democratic standards has become inextricably linked to foreign policy, specifically regarding the EU’s aid packages to Ukraine. While decision-making within the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) has traditionally been viewed as an "insulated" process, our findings reveal an increasing instrumentalization of foreign policy to extract concessions in internal EU disputes. Ultimately, this case illustrates the broader "domestication" of foreign policy by populist actors, where international security matters are leveraged as bargaining chips in domestic and intra-institutional power struggles.
